title: Java Spring Engineer Assessment
description: Mid-to-senior level evaluation focusing on Spring Framework fundamentals, Spring Boot auto-configuration, Spring Data JPA, Spring Security, REST API design, transaction management, AOP, testing strategies, and common Spring integration patterns.

questions.text: What is the Spring Framework's Inversion of Control (IoC) container?
options: A container that manages object creation, configuration, and lifecycle through dependency injection
A web server for Spring applications
A testing framework for Spring components
questions.explanation: The IoC container (ApplicationContext) is responsible for instantiating, configuring, and assembling beans, managing their entire lifecycle from creation to destruction.

questions.text: Which annotation is used to mark a class as a configuration class that defines Spring beans?
questions.explanation: @Configuration indicates that a class declares bean definitions via @Bean-annotated methods. It's processed by the Spring container to generate beans.

questions.text: What is the difference between @Component and @Bean?
options: @Component is used on classes with component scanning; @Bean is used on methods in @Configuration classes for explicit bean registration
@Component is for XML configuration only; @Bean is for annotations
@Component is for singletons; @Bean is for prototypes only
questions.explanation: @Component (and its specializations @Service, @Repository, @Controller) are class-level annotations for auto-detection. @Bean is a method-level annotation used to explicitly declare beans, often for third-party classes.

questions.text: What does the @Autowired annotation do when placed on a constructor?
options: It creates a new instance of the dependency
It tells Spring to inject dependencies via that constructor (constructor injection)
It throws an exception at runtime
It only works with primitive types
questions.explanation: Constructor injection is the recommended approach for mandatory dependencies. Spring calls the constructor with the required beans, making the class immutable and testable.

questions.text: What is the default scope of a Spring bean?
questions.explanation: Singleton is the default scope, meaning one instance is created and shared across the entire Spring container. For web apps, request/session scopes are also available.

questions.text: What is Spring Boot's auto-configuration?
options: Automatic code generation for all classes
Spring Boot automatically configures beans based on dependencies in the classpath (e.g., DataSource if H2 is present)
questions.explanation: @EnableAutoConfiguration (included in @SpringBootApplication) automatically configures Spring based on jar dependencies added to the classpath, reducing boilerplate configuration.

questions.text: Which file is commonly used to externalize configuration in Spring Boot?
questions.explanation: application.properties (or application.yml) is the primary configuration file for Spring Boot, supporting property externalization, profiles, and environment-specific overrides.

questions.text: What is the purpose of @SpringBootTest annotation?
options: To deploy the application to production
To load the full application context for integration testing
questions.explanation: @SpringBootTest boots the entire Spring context for integration testing, allowing real dependency injection and full application behavior validation.

questions.text: Which JPA annotation specifies the mapping between an entity and a database table?
questions.explanation: @Table defines the table name in the database. @Entity marks the class as an entity, but @Table provides specific table mapping details (name, schema, catalog).

questions.text: What does the @Transactional annotation do in Spring?
options: It ensures a method executes within a database transaction, with automatic rollback on runtime exceptions
questions.explanation: @Transactional declares transactional boundaries. Spring wraps the method with a proxy that begins, commits, or rolls back transactions based on exception type.

questions.text: What is the propagation level of @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)?
options: Suspends current transaction and starts a new one
Throws exception if no transaction exists
questions.explanation: REQUIRES_NEW always starts a new transaction, suspending any existing one. The outer and inner transactions are independent; failure in one doesn't affect the other.

questions.text: What is the purpose of Spring Data JPA's derived query methods?
options: To automatically generate queries based on method naming conventions (e.g., findByEmail(String email))
questions.explanation: Derived query methods parse method names at startup and generate JPQL queries automatically, reducing boilerplate code for common queries.

questions.text: What is the difference between @RequestParam and @PathVariable?
options: @RequestParam extracts query parameters (?id=123); @PathVariable extracts URI template variables (/users/{id})
@RequestParam is for POST; @PathVariable is for GET
questions.explanation: @RequestParam binds to query parameters or form data. @PathVariable binds to placeholders in the URI pattern. Both support required/optional settings.

questions.text: What does @ResponseBody annotation do in a Spring Controller?
options: It returns an HTML view name
It writes the return value directly to the HTTP response body (e.g., JSON/XML) instead of resolving to a view
questions.explanation: @ResponseBody tells Spring to skip view resolution and directly serialize the return object to the response body using HttpMessageConverters (e.g., MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter for JSON).

questions.text: What is the purpose of @ControllerAdvice?
options: To define a controller's base path
To handle exceptions globally across multiple controllers
To map HTTP requests to controller methods
questions.explanation: @ControllerAdvice centralizes exception handling, binding, and model attributes for all controllers, reducing duplicate @ExceptionHandler code.

questions.text: What is Spring AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) used for?
options: Separating cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, transactions) from business logic
questions.explanation: AOP allows defining aspects (e.g., @Transactional, @Cacheable) that apply behavior across multiple methods without modifying their code, improving modularity.

questions.text: What is the @Profile annotation used for in Spring?
options: To conditionally enable beans based on active Spring profiles (e.g., dev, test, prod)
questions.explanation: @Profile allows you to register beans only when specific profiles are active, enabling environment-specific configurations (e.g., H2 for dev, PostgreSQL for prod).

questions.text: What does Spring Security's @PreAuthorize annotation do?
options: It pre-authorizes HTTP requests before sending to the client
It defines method-level security using SpEL expressions (e.g., @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')"))
It pre-loads user roles from database
questions.explanation: @PreAuthorize evaluates a SpEL expression before method execution. If false, AccessDeniedException is thrown. Requires @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity.

questions.text: What is the default password encoding strategy in modern Spring Security (5.0+)?
questions.explanation: Spring Security 5+ defaults to BCryptPasswordEncoder, which uses a computationally expensive, salt-embedded hash that resists brute-force attacks.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the @Cacheable annotation?
options: To cache the result of a method invocation, so subsequent calls return cached value without re-execution
questions.explanation: @Cacheable caches method results. On invocation, Spring checks the cache first. If present, returns cached value; otherwise executes method and stores result.

questions.text: What is the difference between @RestController and @Controller?
options: @Controller is for traditional web apps returning views; @RestController combines @Controller and @ResponseBody for REST APIs
questions.explanation: @RestController is a convenience annotation that automatically adds @ResponseBody to every handler method, making it ideal for stateless REST APIs returning JSON/XML.

questions.text: What is the purpose of Spring's @EventListener annotation?
options: To listen to application events (published via ApplicationEventPublisher) for loosely coupled communication
questions.explanation: @EventListener marks a method to handle application events. Combined with @Async, it enables asynchronous, decoupled event handling within the Spring context.

questions.text: What is the difference between @MockBean and @Mock in Spring Boot tests?
options: @Mock is from Mockito; @MockBean creates a mock and replaces any existing bean of the same type in the Spring context
questions.explanation: @Mock (Mockito) creates a plain mock without Spring integration. @MockBean (Spring Boot test) creates a mock and replaces the real bean in the ApplicationContext for integration tests.

questions.text: What does the @Lazy annotation do in Spring?
options: It delays bean initialization until first requested instead of at application startup
questions.explanation: @Lazy prevents eager initialization of a bean. The bean is created only when first requested, reducing startup time and avoiding circular dependency issues.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the @Conditional annotation family (e.g., @ConditionalOnProperty)?
options: To conditionally include beans only when specific conditions are met (e.g., a property exists)
questions.explanation: @Conditional annotations (Spring Boot) control bean registration based on conditions like classpath presence, property values, or bean existence. Core to auto-configuration.
